titleStmt.title: SII 1.153: original edition by Eugen Hultzsch
PART IV. ADDENDA. No. 153. ON A JAINA TEMPLE AT VIJAYANAGARA.

summary.p: Next to No. 152, this is the oldest dated inscription at Vijayanagara. It is engraved on both sides of the north-west entrance of a ruined Jaina temple, which is situated to the south-west of the temple No. 35 on the Madras Survey Map. A careless transcript and paraphrase in the Asiatic ResearchesVol. XX, p. 38 and pp. 22 ff. has been useful so far as it enabled Mr. R.Sewell to complete the pedigree of the first Vijayanagara dynasty in his Lists of Antiquities.Vol. II, p. 244.

The inscription is written in large and handsome characters, which are, however, considerably obliterated in consequence of the usual coating with chunnam. It records, in Sanskrit, prose and verse, that in the ParńĀbhava year, which was current after the expiration of the ŇöńĀka year 1348 (line 25), king DevarńĀja II. built a stone-temple (chaityńĀlaya or chaityńĀgńĀra) of the Arhat PńĀrŇõvanńĀtha (l. 5) or PńĀrŇõva-JineŇõvara (l. 27) in a street (vńęthi) of the PńĀn-supńĀrńę BńĀzńĀr (Kramuka-parŠĻáńĀpaŠĻáa, l. 4, or ParŠĻáa-pŇęgńęphalńĀpaŠĻáa, l. 25) at his residence Vijayanagara (l. 4) or Vijayanagarńę (l. 6), which belonged to the KarŠĻáńĀŠĻ≠a country (ll. 4 and 6).
The chief value of the inscription consists in the pedigree, which it gives no less than three times,Lines 1 to 4; lines 8 to 15; and line 24, verse 15. of the first Vijayanagara dynasty:‚ÄĒ
1. Bukka (ll. 1, 9, 24) of the race of Yadu (Yadu-kula, l. 8, or YńĀdavńĀnvaya, l. 1).
5. His son, DevarńĀja (II.) (ll. 15, 18, 19, 20, 22, 24), Abhinava-DevarńĀja (ll. 3 f.), or Vńęra-DevarńĀja (l. 16), mahńĀrńĀja (l. 4), rńĀjńĀdhirńĀja, rńĀjaparameŇõvara, etc. (ll. 3 and 23).

In the subjoined genealogical table of the first or YńĀdava dynasty of Vijayanagara, the names of the father and of the elder brother of Bukka and those Ňöaka dates, for which no references are given in the foot-notes, are taken from Mr. Fleet's table of the same dynasty.Jour. Bo. Br. R. A. S. Vol. XII, p. 339.

[[genealogical table]] [[L1C1]] SaŠĻĀgama. [[L2C1]] Harihara I. (Ňöaka 1261.Indian Antiquary, Vol. X, p. 63.) [[L2C2]] Bukka. (Ňöaka 1276 [current], 1277, 1278, 1290.) [[L3C1]] Harihara II. (Ňöaka 1301, 1307,This is the date of No. 152, above. 1317,Colebrooke's Miscellaneous Essays, Madras, 1872, Vol. II, p. 259. 1321.) [[L4C1]] DevarńĀja I. (Ňöaka 1332, 1334.) [[L5C1]] Vijaya.Mr. Sewell, Lists, Vol. I, p. 207, mentions an inscription of this king, which is dated in Ňöaka 1340 [expired]. [[L6C1]] DevarńĀja II. (Ňöaka 1346, 1347, 1348, 1353 [current], 1371.The four last dates are those of Nos. 56, 153, 54 and 81, respectively, of this volume.)

During the reign of DevarńĀja II. the city of Vijayanagara was visited by ‚ÄėAbdu‚ÄôrrazzńĀq as an ambassador of Sult6ńĀn ShńĀh Rukh of Samarkand, a son of the great TńęmŇęr. ‚ÄėAbdu‚Äôr-razzńĀq informs us, that he stayed at BńęjńĀnagar (Vijayanagara), the capital of Deo RńĀńę (DevarńĀja II.), from the close of Z6u'l-Šł•ijja A.H. 846 = end of April A.D. 1443 to the 12th Sha'bńĀn A.H. 847 = 5th December A.D. 1443.The Hijra dates are converted into Christian ones according to Dr. W√ľstenfeld's Vergleichungs-Tabellen, Leipzig, 1854. An English translation of his own account of his journey is included in Elliot and Dowson's History of India.Vol. IV, pp. 95 ff. Curiously enough, the whole is also incorporated with slight alterations in Galland's translation of the Thousand and One Nights, where it forms part of the Story of Prince AŠł•mad and the Fairy Parńę BńĀnŇę. This is one of the twelve doubtful stories, the originals of which are not found in the existing Arabic MSS. of the Nights. The late Professor WeilPreface to his German translation, 3rd edition, p. ii. was of opinion, that they were probably contained in the fourth volume of the Paris MS., which was lost after Galland's death; and two of the missing stories have since been actually recovered by M. Zotenberg.See Lady Burton's Arabian Nights, Vol. VI, p. 248, note 2; Tr√ľbner's Record, New Series, Vol. IX, p. 65. In ‚ÄėAbdu‚Äôr-razzńĀq's account of Vijayanagara, we possess the dated original, from which part of the Story of Prince AŠł•mad was taken. In the absence of works of reference, I cannot say if this fact,‚ÄĒwhich furnishes us with a terminus a quo for the compilation of that story,‚ÄĒhas been noticed before.

According to ‚ÄėAbdu‚Äôr-razzńĀq,L. c. p. 109. DevarńĀja II. issued the following coins:‚ÄĒI. Gold: (1) varńĀha; (2) partńĀb = (1/2) varńĀha; (3) fanam = (1/10) partńĀb. II. Silver: tńĀr = (1/6) fanam. III. Copper: jńętal = (1/3) tńĀr. Pagodas or varńĀhas with the legend ŇõrńępratńĀpadevarńĀya, which on some copies is corrupted into ŇõrńępratńĀpadńĀvarńĀya, are described by Dr. Bidie,J. As. Soc. Bengal, Vol. LI, pp. 43 f., No. 11. The legend of No. 10a (Elliot's No. 99) is not ŇõrńępratńĀpadevarńĀya, but ŇõrńępratńĀpńĀcyutarńĀya; Elliot's No. 98 reads ŇõrńępratńĀpacyutarńĀya (sic). who also figures a pagoda of Bukka.Ibid. p. 42, No. 9.The name partńĀb, which ‚ÄėAbdu‚Äôr-razzńĀq attributes to the half pagoda, is probably connected with the surname PratńĀpa, which occurs before the names of Vijayanagara kings both on coins and in inscriptions. Dr. Bain of Bangalore possesses a half pagodaA half pagoda, which reads ŇõrńępratńĀpaharihara and probably refers to Harihara II., is figured in Sir W. Elliot's Coins of Southern India, Plate iii, Nos. 96 and 97. with the legend ŇõrńępratńĀpadovarńĀya (sic) and of the same type as the corresponding pagoda. Two quarter pagodas in my cabinet have on the obverse an elephant which faces the left, and on the reverse the legend ŇõrńędevarńĀya. No fanam or silver coin with DevarńĀja's name has been hitherto discovered. Copper coins of DevarńĀja are very common in the South-Indian bńĀzńĀrs. They have on the obverse a bull or an elephant, and on the reverse the legends ŇõrńędevarńĀya, pratńĀpadevarńĀya, rńĀyagajagaŠĻĀŠłćabheruŠĻĀŠłća, or ŇõrńęnńęlakaŠĻĀŠĻ≠ha.That the coins, which bear the two last-mentioned legends, belong to DevarńĀja, may be concluded from his Kanarese or NńĀgarńę initial de, which is engraved on the obverse.

revisionDesc.change: Further conversion of digital encoding to DHARMA encoding scheme according to EGD (Encoding Guide for Diplomatic Editions)
Conversion of digital encoding to DHARMA encoding scheme according to EGD (Encoding Guide for Diplomatic Editions)

div.p:
A. To the left of the entrance.
Ňõubhamastu . ŇõrńęmatparamagaŠĻĀbhńęrasyńĀdvńĀdńĀmoghalńĀŠĻĀthchanaŠĻĀm¬∑ . jńęyńĀttrailokyanńĀthasya ŇõńĀsanaŠĻĀ jinaŇõńĀsanaŠĻĀ . 1 ŇõrńęmadyńĀdavńĀnvayńĀrnnŠĻáavapŇęrnnŠĻáacaŠĻĀdrasya . ŇõrńębukkapŠĻõthvńębhujaŠł• puŠĻĀŠĻáyaparipńĀ
kapariŠĻáatamŇęrttessŠĻõtkńęrtterhariharamahńĀrńĀjasya paryyńĀyńĀvatńĀrńĀddhńęrńĀddevarńĀjanareŇõvarńĀddevarńĀjńĀdiva vijayaŇõrńęvńęravijayanruŠĻõpatissaŠĻĀjńĀtast
smńĀdrohaŠĻáńĀdreriva mahńĀmńĀŠĻáikyakńĀŠĻĀŠłćo nńętipratńĀpasthirńękŠĻõtasńĀmrńĀjyasiŠĻĀhńĀsanaŠł• . rńĀjńĀdhirńĀjarńĀjaparameŇõvarńĀdibirudavikhyńĀto guŠĻáanidhirabhi
navadevarńĀjamahńĀrńĀjo nijńĀj√ĪńĀparipńĀlitakarnnńĀŠĻ≠adeŇõamadhyavarttinaŠł• svńĀvńĀsabhŇętavijayanagarasya kramukaparnnŠĻáńĀpaŠĻáavńęthyńĀmńĀcaŠĻĀdratńĀramńĀtmakńę
rttidharmmapravŠĻõttaye . sakalaj√ĪńĀnasńĀmrńĀjyavirńĀjamńĀnasya syńĀdvńĀdavidyńĀprakaŠĻ≠anapaŠĻ≠ńęyasaŠł• pńĀrŇõvanńĀthasyńĀrhataŠł• ŇõilńĀmayaŠĻĀ caityńĀlayamacńękarat¬∑ .
deŇõaŠł• karŠĻáńĀŠĻ≠anńĀmńĀbhŇędńĀvńĀsaŠł• sarvvasaŠĻĀpadńĀŠĻĀ . viŠłćaŠĻĀbayati yaŠł• svarggaŠĻĀ puroŠłćńĀŇõńĀŇõanńĀŇõrayaŠĻĀ . 2 vijayanagarńęti tasminnagarńę nagarńęti
ramyaharmyńĀste . nagariŠĻ£u nagarńę yasyńĀ na garńęyasyeva gurubhiraiŇõvaryyaiŠł• . 3 kanakojvalasńĀlaraŇõmijńĀlaiŠł• parikhńĀŠĻĀbupratibiŠĻĀvitairalaŠĻĀ yńĀ
vasudheva vibhńĀti bńĀŠłćabńĀrccirvŠĻõtaratrńĀkaramekhalńĀparńętńĀ . . ŇõrńęmńĀnuddńĀmadhńĀmńĀ yadukulatilakassńĀrasauŠĻĀdaryyasńęmńĀ dhńęmńĀnrńĀmńĀ
bhirńĀmńĀkŠĻõtiravanitale bhńĀti bhńĀgyńĀttabhŇęmńĀ . vikrńĀŠĻĀtyńĀkrńĀŠĻĀtadikko vimatadharaŠĻáibhŠĻõtpaŠĻĀkajaŇõreŠĻáivikkaŠł• . kŠĻ£oŠĻáyńĀŠĻĀ jńĀgartti bukkakŠĻ£itipati
raribhŇębhŠĻõtcchiraŇõchitpŠĻõŠĻ£atkaŠł• . 4 tatprńĀptńĀtmńĀvatńĀra sphurati hariharakŠĻ£mńĀpatirj√ĪńĀtasńĀro dńĀridryasphńĀravńĀrńĀkarataraŠĻáavidhau viŠĻ£phuratkarnnŠĻáadhńĀraŠł• . bhŇę
dńĀnasvarnnŠĻáadńĀnńĀnukŠĻõtaparaŇõughŠĻõtpadminńębaŠĻĀdhusŇęnu sphńĀrńĀkŇępńĀratńęrńĀvaŠł∑inihitajayastaŠĻĀbhavinyastakńęrttiŠł• . 5 tenńĀjanyarirńĀjatallajaŇõira
stomasphuracchekharapratyuptopaladńępikńĀpariŠĻáamatpńĀdńĀbjanńęrńĀjanaŠł• . vidvatkairavamaŠĻĀŠłćalńęhimakaro vikhyńĀtavńęryyńĀkaraŠł• ŇõreyńĀnvńęrara
mńĀsvayaŠĻĀvŠĻõtavaraŠł• ŇõrńędevarńĀjeŇõvaraŠł• . 6 tajjanmńĀsminvadńĀnyo jagati vijayate puŠĻĀŠĻáyacńĀritramńĀŠĻĀnyo dńĀnadhvastńĀrtthidainyo vijayanarapatiŠł• khaŠĻĀ
ŠłćitńĀrńĀtisainyaŠł• . pratyudyajjaitrayńĀtrńĀsamasamayasamudbhŇętaketuprasŇętasphńĀyadvńĀtyopahatyńĀpratihatavimataughapratńĀpapradńępaŠł• . 7
B. To the right of the entrance.
tasmńĀdasmńĀji√ĪjitńĀtmńĀjani jagati yathńĀ jaŠĻĀbhajeturjjayaŠĻĀto rńĀjńĀ ŇõrńędevarńĀjo vijayanŠĻõpativńĀrńĀŇõirńĀkńĀŇõaŇõńĀŠĻĀkaŠł• . kopńĀŠĻ≠opapravŠĻõttaprabalaraŠĻáamiladvipratńępakŠĻ£a
mńĀpaprńĀŠĻáaŇõreŠĻáńęnabhasvannivahakabalanavyagrakhaŠłćgorageŠĻĀdraŠł• . 8 vńęraŇõrńędevarńĀjo vijayanŠĻõpatapassńĀrasaŠĻĀjńĀtamŇęrttirbbharttńĀ bhŇęmervvibhńĀti praŠĻáatariputaterńĀrttijńĀtasya harttńĀ .
krŇęrakrodheddhayuddhoddhurakaraŠĻ≠ighaŠĻ≠ńĀkarŠĻáŠĻáaŇõŇęrppaprasarppadvńĀtavrńĀtopaghńĀtapratihatavimatńĀdabhradhruŠĻõtyabhrasaŠĻĀghaŠł• . 9 yaddhńĀŠĻ≠ńęghoraghoŠĻ≠ńękhuradalitadharńĀreŠĻáubhirvvńęryyavahnerddhŇę
mastomńĀyamńĀnaiŠł• pratinŠĻõpatigaŠĻáastrńędŠĻõŇõaŠł• sńĀŇõrudhńĀrńĀŠł• . prodyaddarppaprabhŇętapratibhaŠĻ≠asubhaŠĻ≠ńĀsphoŠĻ≠anńĀŠĻ≠opajńĀgradroŠĻ£otkarŠĻ£ńĀŠĻĀdhakńĀradyumaŠĻáirudayate devarńĀjeŇõvaroyaŠĻĀ . 10
viŇõvasminvijayakŠĻ£itńęŇõajanuŠĻ£aŠł• ŇõrńędevarńĀjeŇõiturllakŠĻ£mńęŠĻĀ kńęrttisitńĀŠĻĀbujaŠĻĀ kalayate ŇõauryyńĀkhyasŇęryyodayńĀt . ńÄŇõńĀ yatra palńĀŇõatńĀmupagatńĀŠł•
svarŠĻáŠĻáńĀcalaŠł• karŠĻáŠĻáikńĀ bhŠĻõŠĻĀgńĀ dikŠĻ£u mataŠĻĀgajńĀ jaladhayo mńĀraŠĻĀdabiŠĻĀdŇętkarńĀŠł• . 11 vikhyńĀte vijayńĀtmaje vitarati ŇõrńędevarńĀjeŇõvare karŠĻáŠĻáasyńĀjani va
rŠĻáŠĻáanńĀ vigalitńĀ vńĀcyńĀ dadhńęcyńĀdayaŠł• . meghńĀnńĀmapi moghatńĀ pariŠĻáatńĀ ciŠĻĀtńĀ na ciŠĻĀtńĀmaŠĻáeŠł• svalpńĀŠł• kalpamahńęruhńĀŠł• prathayate svarŠĻáaicikńę nńęcatńĀŠĻĀ . 12
soyaŠĻĀ kńęrttisarasvatńęvasumatńęvńĀŠĻáńęvadhŇębhissamaŠĻĀ bhavyo dńęvyati devarńĀjanŠĻõpatirbbhŇędevadivyaddrumaŠł• . yaŇõŇõaurirbbaliyńĀcanńĀvirahittaŇõcaŠĻĀdraŠł• kaŠł∑aŠĻĀ
kojjhitaŠł• ŇõakrassatyamagotrabhiddinakaraŇõcńĀsatpathollaŠĻĀghanaŠł• . 13 madanamanoharamŇęrttiŠł• mahiŠł∑ńĀjanamńĀnasńĀrasaŠĻĀharaŠĻáaŠł• . rńĀjńĀdhirńĀjarńĀjńĀdimapadaparameŇõvarńĀdini
jabirudaŠł• . 14 Ňõaktau bukkamahńępńĀlo dńĀne harihareŇõvaraŠł• . Ňõauryye ŇõrńędevarńĀjeŇõo j√ĪńĀne vijayabhŇępatiŠł• . 15 soyaŠĻĀ ŇõrńędevarńĀjeŇõo vidyńĀvinayaviŇõrutaŠł• . prńĀ
guktapuravńęthyaŠĻĀtaŠł• parŠĻáŠĻáapŇęgńęphalńĀpaŠĻáe . 16 ŇõńĀkebde pramite yńĀte vasusiŠĻĀdhuguŠĻáeŠĻĀdubhiŠł• . parńĀbhavńĀbde kńĀrttikyńĀŠĻĀ dharmmakńęrttipravŠĻõttaye . 17 syńĀ
dvńĀdamatasamartthanakharvvitadurvvńĀdigarvvavńĀgvitateŠł• . AŠĻ£ŠĻ≠ńĀdaŇõadoŠĻ£amahńĀmadagajanikuruŠĻĀbamahitamŠĻõgarńĀjaŠł• . 18 bhavyńĀŠĻĀbhoruhabhńĀnoriŠĻĀdrńĀdisu
reŠĻĀdravŠĻõŠĻĀdavaŠĻĀdyasya . muktivadhŇępriyabharttuŠł• ŇõrńępńĀrŇõvajineŇõvarasya karuŠĻáńĀbdheŠł• . 19 bhavyaparitoŠĻ£ahetuŠĻĀ ŇõilńĀmayaŠĻĀ setumakhiladharmmasya . caityńĀgńĀramacńękara
dńĀdharaŠĻáidyumaŠĻáihimakarasthairyyaŠĻĀ . 20

app.note: As this inscription makes scarcely any distinction between the secondary forms of i and ńę, this word might as well be nagarńęŠĻ£u. I have adopted the reading nagariŠĻ£u for the sake of the metre, though the form nagari is not found in the dictionaries.

div.p: Let there be prosperity! (Verse 1.) May the religion of the lord of the three worlds, the religion of Jina, the unfailing characteristic of which is the glorious and extremely mysterious scepticism, be victorious!This verse is identical with verse 2 of No. 152.

(Line 1.) The victorious and illustrious prince Vńęra-Vijaya sprang from the brave prince DevarńĀja (I.), who resembled the king of the gods and who was descended in his turn from the glorious mahńĀrńĀja Harihara (II.), whose body was produced by the results of the good deeds of the illustrious king Bukka, who, just as the full-moon from the ocean, (rose) from the illustrious YńĀdava race (YńĀdavńĀnvaya). The virtuous mahńĀrńĀja Abhinava-DevarńĀja (i.e., the young DevarńĀja, or DevarńĀja II.),‚ÄĒ(who sprang) from this (Vńęra-Vijaya), just as a heap of large rubies from the RohaŠĻáa mountain,This is the Adam's Peak in Ceylon. who made the throne of his empire firm by polity and valour, and who was known by the surnames of rńĀjńĀdhirńĀja, rńĀjaparameŇõvara, etc.,‚ÄĒin order that his fame and merit might last as long as the moon and the stars,‚ÄĒcaused a temple (chaityńĀlaya) of stone to be built to the Arhat PńĀrŇõvanńĀtha,‚ÄĒ who rules over the empire of all knowledge, and who well knew how to proclaim the doctrine of scepticism (syńĀdvńĀda-vidyńĀSee page 158, note 1, and compare syńĀdvńĀda-mata in lines 25 f. of the present inscription.),‚ÄĒin a street of the PńĀn-supńĀrńę BńĀzńĀr (KramukaparŠĻáńĀpaŠĻáa) at his (the king's) residence Vijayanagara, that was situated in the midst of (the country called) KarŠĻáńĀŠĻ≠a-deŇõa, which was protected by his orders.

(Verse 2.) There was a country (deŇõa), KarŠĻáńĀŠĻ≠a by name, which was the abode of all wealth, and which equalled heaven, the seat of the gods.With puroŠłćńĀŇõńĀŇõana, ‚Äúan eater of the sacrificial cake,‚ÄĚ ‚Äúa god,‚ÄĚ compare the synonym puroŠłćńĀŇõabhui in the ŇöiŇõupńĀlavadha, sarga ii, verse 106.
(Verse 3.) In this (country) there is a city, called Vijayanagarńę, whose lovely palaces are as high as mountains, and than which none among the cities is more important in great power.

(Line 7.) Through the mass of the rays, (which issue from) its golden walls, and which are reflected in the water of its moat, this (city) closely resembles the earth, that is surrounded by the girdle of the ocean, which is encircled by the lustre of the submarine fire (bńĀŠłćabaThe same spelling of the word is found in the Kanarese and Telugu dictionaries. Dr. Gundert's MalayńĀŠł∑am Dictionary has baŠłćavńĀgni and vaŠłćavńĀgni, Winslow's Tamil Dictionary pńĀŠĻ≠apam, pńĀŠĻ≠avam and vaŠĻ≠avńĀkkiŠĻČi.).

(Verse 4.) The illustrious, brilliant and wise king Bukka,‚ÄĒwho is the ornament of the race of Yadu (Yadu-kula), who has reached the highest point of power and beauty, whose appearance is as lovely as that of RńĀma, who has acquired wealth by his good fortune, who has subdued (all) quarters by his valour, (who crushes) the crowd of rival kings, just as a young elephantVikka, ‚Äúan elephant twenty years old‚ÄĚ (Sanderson's Canarese Dictionary), ‚Äúa young elephant‚ÄĚ (Brewn's Telugu Dictionary). a group of lotuses, and whose arrows split the heads of the kings of his enemies,‚ÄĒshines on earth (and) watches over it.

(Verse 5.) Resplendent is his son, king Harihara (II.), whose strength is well-known, (who has proved) a splendid helmsman in crossing the great ocean of poverty, who has equalled the bearer of the axeI.e., ParaŇõurńĀma, who gave the earth to KaŇõyapa. by his gifts of land and the son of the sunI.e., KarŠĻáa, the son of SŇęrya by Kuntńę. by his gifts of gold, and who has deposited his fame in pillars of victory (jayastambha), which he erected in an uninterrupted line on the shore of the great ocean.

(Verse 6.) From him sprang the most excellent and illustrious lord DevarńĀja (I.), the worship (nńęrńĀjana) of whose lotus-feet was performed with a lamp, (that consisted of) the precious stones, which were set in the glittering diadems on the multitude of the heads of the excellentThe word tallaja, which is mentioned in the AmarakoŇõa and in the Kanarese and Telugu dictionaries, has not yet been met with in Sanskrit literature. kings of his enemiesI.e., in the case of DevarńĀja I., the jewels on the bowing heads of conquered kings did the duty of the lamp, which is waved before an idol in the nńęrńĀjana (also called ńĀrńĀtrika) ceremony.; (who gladdened) the learned, just as the moon the night-lotuses; who was a mine of well-known prowess; and who was voluntarily chosen as husband by (Lakshmńę) the mistress of heroes.

(Verse 7.) Victorious in this world is his son, the liberal prince Vijaya, who is to be respected on account of his pious deeds, who has put an end to the distress of beggars by his gifts, who has crushed the armies of his foes, and the light of the courage of whose numerous enemies was extinguished by the (mere) touch of the violentThe root sphńĀy is here used in the parasmaipada, though the DhńĀtupńĀŠĻ≠ha enumerates it among the anudńĀttetaŠł•. wind, that was produced by his banners, which were raised (or: by the comet, which rose) at the very moment of the starting of his victorious expeditions.

(Verse 8.) Just as Jayanta from (InŠłćra) the conqueror of (the demon) Jambha, and just as the full-moon from the ocean, there was born in this world from that prince Vijaya the passionless and illustrious king DevarńĀja (II.), whose sword was engaged in destroying numbers of lives,‚ÄĒjust as the king of serpents is engaged in swallowing masses of wind,‚ÄĒThe snakes are supposed to subsist on wind and are therefore called pavanńĀŇõanńĀŠł•, ‚Äúthe eaters of wind.‚ÄĚ of rival kings, who met (him) in mighty battles, which were fought with excessive fury.

(Verse 9.) Resplendent is the lord of the earth, the illustrious Vńęra-DevarńĀja (II.), whose body was produced by the power of the austerities of prince Vijaya; who removed the great distress of the crowd of his prostrated enemies (by pardoning them); and whose enemies' great fortitude,‚ÄĒas a mass of clouds,‚ÄĒwas scattered by the (mere) touch of the violent wind, that was produced by (the flapping of) the ears,‚ÄĒwhich resembled winnowing-baskets,‚ÄĒof the troop of his elephants, who were longing for battles, that raged with fierce fury.

(Verse 10.) (Ever) rising is this lord DevarńĀja (II.), the eyes of the wives of the crowd of whose rival kings are filled with showers of tears,‚ÄĒas if it were by the dense smoke of the fire of (his) prowess,‚ÄĒby the dust, (which rises from) the earth, that is split by the hoofs of his steeds, which are terrible in their attack; and who, just as the sun (dispels) darkness, (subdues) the excessive anger,‚ÄĒwhich is indefatigable in bold challenges,‚ÄĒThe technical meaning of ńĀsphoŠĻ≠ana is: ‚Äústriking the upper part of the left arm, the lower part of which is folded over the chest, with the right hand, as a sign of defiance.‚ÄĚ This is even now done by wrestlers. of many brave and daring warriors of the opposite party.

(Verse 11.) In consequence of the rising of the sun, which is called the prowess of the illustrious lord DevarńĀja (II.), the son of king Vijaya, there spreads its splendour over the whole world the white lotus-flower of his fame, in which the points of the compass are the petals, the golden mountain (Meru) the seed-vessel,KarŠĻáikńĀchala, ‚Äúthe mountain of the seed-vessel,‚ÄĚ is one of the names of Meru; SvarŠĻáńĀchala is a synonym of HemńĀdri, ‚Äúthe golden mountain,‚ÄĚ another name of Meru. the elephants of the quarters the bees, and the oceans so many drops of honey.MńĀranda, an adjective from maranda, is not found in the dictionaries and seems to be formed by the composer of the inscription, in order to satisfy the exigencies of the metre.

(Verse 12.) Since the famous and illustrious lord DevarńĀja (II.), the son of Vijaya, is making gifts, the praise of KarŠĻáa has ceased; DadhńęchiAccording to the MahńĀbhńĀrata, Dadhńęcha (alias Dadhńęchi) gave up his bones, from which a thunderbolt was formed to kill VŠĻõitra; see Bo7htlingk and Roth's Sanskrit Dictionary, s.v. and others are worthy of blame; even the clouds (megha) have turned useless (mogha); nobody thinks of the thinking-jewel (chintńĀmaŠĻái); the kalpa-trees appear very small (alpa); and the heavenly cow (naichikńę) confesses her inferiority (nńęchatńĀ).

(Verse 13.) This excellent prince DevarńĀja (II.), (who resembles) the tree of heaven (by his liberality) to BrńĀhmaŠĻáas, is sporting with his queens, (viz.) the river of (his) fame, the earth and the goddess of speech. Verily, he resembles Ňöauri (VishŠĻáu), but has not to beg for his revenue (bali), (while VishŠĻáu in his dwarf-incarnation begged land from Bali); he resembles the moon, but is spotless; he resembles Ňöakra (Indra), but does not destroy families (gotra), (while Indra split the mountains:‚ÄĒgotra);The same play on the two meanings of gotrabhid occurs in KalhaŠĻáa's RńĀjataraŠĻĀgiŠĻáńę, taraŠĻĀga i, verse 92. and he resembles the sun, but never transgresses the right course, (while the sun daily changes his course in the sky).

(Verse 14.) His form is as lovely as that of Cupid, and he overcomes the great pride of women. His own surnames (biruda) are rńĀjńĀdhirńĀja, rńĀjaparameŇõvara,rńĀjńĀdimapadaparameŇõvara means literally: ‚Äú(a compound ending in) parameŇõvara, the first member of which is rńĀjan.‚ÄĚ Similar ‚Äėpoetical‚Äô expressions are dhanurupapado vedaŠł• in the KirńĀtńĀrjunńęya, sarga xviii; verse 44; hiraŠĻáyapŇęrvaŠł• kaŇõipuŠł• in the ŇöiŇõupńĀlavadha, sarga i, verse 42; devapŇęrvo giriŠł• in the MeghadŇęta, verse 42; daŇõapŇęrvarathaŠł• and rńĀjopapadaŠĻĀ niŇõńĀntam in the RaghuvaŠĻĀŇõa, sarga viii, verse 29, and sarga xvi, verse 40.etc.

(Verse 15.) In power, he resembles king Bukka, in liberality‚ÄĒthe lord Harihara (II.), in prowess‚ÄĒthe illustrious lord DevarńĀja (I.), and in wisdom‚ÄĒking Vijaya.This verse contains the names of the predecessors of DevarńĀja II. in chronological order, and may thus be considered as a third copy of the pedigree, which was given twice before, once in prose (lines 1 to 4) and once in verse (lines 8 to 15.)

(Verse 16 to 20.) This illustrious lord DevarńĀja (II.), who was famed both for wisdom and modesty, caused to be built in a street of the above-mentioned city,Viz., Vijayanagarńę; see verse 3. in the PńĀn-supńĀrńę BńĀzńĀr (ParŠĻáa-pŇęgńęphalńĀpaŠĻáa), when the ŇöńĀka year measured by the Vasus (8), the oceans (4), the qualities (3) and the moon (1) had passed, in the (cyclic) year ParńĀbhava, on KńĀrttikńę (i.e., on the day of the full-moon in the month of KńĀrttika), in order to propagate (his) merit and fame, a temple (chaityńĀgńĀra) of stone, which gives delight to the good, which is a bridge for (his) whole merit, and which shall last as long as the earth, the sun and the moon, to the blessed PńĀrŇõva, the lord of Jinas, who has maimed the arrogant bombast of evil-speakers by establishing the doctrine of scepticism (syńĀdvńĀda-mata), who is celebrated as a lion to the herd of extremely furious elephants:‚ÄĒthe eighteen sins (dosha), who is a sun, (which gladdens) the good, like lotuses, who is to be praised by Indra and all other lords of the gods, who is the beloved husband of the goddess of salvation, and who is an ocean of mercy.
Digital edition of SII 1.153 by converted to DHARMA conventions by Emmanuel Francis.
